---
title: "Big Data, Big Experiments, and Big Problems"
author: "Susan Vanderplas"
date: "Jan 12, 2020"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    self_contained: true
    mathjax: "https://cdn.bootcss.com/mathjax/2.7.1/MathJax.js?config=TeX-MML-AM_HTMLorMML"
    css: ["default", "css/csafe.css", "css/csafe-fonts.css", "css/this-presentation.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---
class: primary
```{r setup, echo = F, cache = F, include = F}
library(magrittr)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
mytheme <- theme_bw() + 
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey50"),
        panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey60"))

theme_set(mytheme)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = F, warning = F, cache = T, dpi = 300, dev = 'png')

```

class:primary,middle,center
## Slides at: 
<h3>srvanderplas.github.io/Presentations/PAG2020/</h3>

---
class:inverse
# Alternate Title: <br/>10 ways to screw up your experiment

---
class:primary
## Disclaimer

.large[All of these examples are real. 

*Most* of the mistakes I discuss are my own or occurred on projects I was a major participant in.
]

--

***
.large[
Everyone screws up. My goal is to ensure that we discover
**new** and **innovative** ways to screw up.
]

---
class:inverse
# \# 1. Jump Right In: Start a GIANT study in a new field

---
class:primary
## Jump Right In: <br/>Longitudinal Shoe Study

- 160 Pairs of shoes, each worn for ~6 months

- 2 shoe models with different patterns and sole materials

- 7 different methods for imaging the shoes

- 3-4 different timepoints collected for each pair of shoes

--

***
Problems:

- Poor experimental technique

- Controlled for the wrong variables

- Data collection methods didn't approximate real life forensic techniques

???

The Center for Statistics and Applications in Forensic Evidence (or CSAFE, for short) was created 5 years ago through a grant from NIST. Before that point, there were relatively few people doing research in statistics and forensics. So when CSAFE started, everyone was pretty new to the field of forensics, even if we were well-trained statisticians. 

Thing is, in many cases, statisticians don't collect their own data. So hopefully, you wouldn't make these mistakes.

We started out by creating a giant study using new-to-us equipment for collecting data. Why not make this study serve multiple purposes, right? It'll be efficient that way.

We varied shoe size, shoe model, collected data on the level of use the shoes got, the height and weight of the participants, the whole deal. We monitored these participants for a semester to see how the wear patterns started to emerge. Sounds great, right?

What we didn't do is refine the data collection methods first. We didn't spend a lot of time consulting with experts in the field until the experiment was underway, only to find out we'd done things sub-optimally.

You'll hear a lot about this experiment during this talk - I've been working with the data for a year or more now, so I'm possibly using this as a combination of group therapy and "scared straight". In my opinion, there were a couple of large errors made in this study, and they can be summarized as "trying to do too much, too soon". Sometimes you have to do the study while you have the money, but we would have been much better off starting with several smaller studies. 


---
class:primary
## Jump Right In: <br/>Milk Nutrition Study

[Lanarkshire Milk Experiment](https://www.jstor.org/stable/2332424) described by Student (of Student's T-test fame)

- 20,000 children spread across 67 schools

- 5,000 given pasteurized milk, 5,000 given raw milk, 10,000 controls

- Several issues:
  - Random assignment problems
  - Weighing children with clothes (winter vs. summer weight)
  - Combining controls for groups (preventing school-wise comparison)

.center[Starting out with smaller studies might have made these issues apparent]

---
class:inverse,center,middle
# \# 2. Pilot studies are a waste of time

---
class:primary
## Pilot studies are a waste <br/>of time

Statisticians: 
- pilot studies to get error estimates (classroom context)
- determine power/study size for the main study

If you don't really need error estimates, and you design your main study to be as big as is feasible, you don't need a pilot study, right?

--

.center[
<img src="https://media.giphy.com/media/1Pfd5qmaqBvjO/giphy.gif" alt="Picard stupid gif"/>
]


---
class:primary
## Pilot studies are a waste <br/>of time

- Pilot studies give you a chance to test your methods, your code, your analysis...

- If everything goes perfectly during the pilot study and you don't change anything afterwards, you can always throw the data into the main study (Phase 1 and Phase 2)
--

> (I've never seen this happen)

---
class:inverse
# \# 3. Data collection for the sake of data collection

---
class:primary
## Data collection for its own sake

Very context dependent.

- Every study should have a goal in mind

- Sometimes, that goal can be stated as a hypothesis

- It's generally not worth collecting the data unless you have a purpose for it.

.center[
<img src="https://media.giphy.com/media/3o7btZ3T6y3JTmjg4w/giphy.gif" alt="Floating in space gif"/>
]

---
class:primary
## Data collection for its<br/>own sake is good

- Purpose-driven data collection

- Exploratory data collection still involves goals  

???
I should start out by asking... do any of you have someone leading your group, your organization, or your section who is enthusiastic to a fault, but tends to not want to get into the details?

CSAFE is about to launch a project named "Project Tread" which is supposed to be primarily an outreach project. With that said, we can't get our director to actually write off the idea of using the data for ... something... at some point in time.

I get it. I'm a packrat too when it comes to data. I have datasets sitting on my machine that are obscure and probably useless, but I can't bring myself to delete them. However, not thinking about what you want to do with the data can lead to some really big problems down the road.

So using this particular project as an example, we forced our project director to come up with the following uses: 

--

Example: Shoe data

  - want a large population of shoe prints to use for testing new matching methods
  - Need to be able to resolve patterns on the shoe
  - Ideally would be able to resolve randomly acquired damage (1mm or smaller)
  - Prints should be smudged and otherwise unclear, just like in a crime scene

--

All of these are goals that inform data collection methods

???

Ideally, we'd still have a specific analysis we would want to do with the collected data. But, even with the really rough goals we have listed above, we changed the protocol of the experiment to make the downstream data processing easier with one simple modification: we're now asking each person to trace the bottom of their shoe and upload that picture with all of the shoe print pictures. That image will serve as a "mask" and help us clean up the other images.

---
class:inverse
# \# 4. You can figure out how to analyze the data after it's collected

---
class:primary
## You can figure out how to analyze the data later

Statistically, this is bad practice because you should set up your hypotheses and analysis methods before you collect the data.

In Data Science, this is bad practice for some additional reasons:

1. You need to know how to clean the data before you can analyze it
  - It's a good idea to test the cleaning pipeline before you collect all of the data

2. How you want to analyze the data may change the methods you use to collect it

3. Pre-testing the analysis pipeline gives you the chance to find bugs (saving time, \$, and retracted publications)

--

If you do the pilot study and analysis... you're already in the clear on this one

---
class:inverse
# \# 5. Don't write the code until after the experiment is complete

---
class:primary
## Don't write the code until<br/>the experiment is complete

.center[
<img src="https://media.giphy.com/media/AFjfPUJ0mjWJG/giphy.gif" alt="rage gif- parks and rec" width="50%" class="center"/>
]

---
class:inverse
# \# 6. Allow your collaborators to change experimental procedures any time

---
class:inverse
# \# 7. Store your data in a proprietary format

---
class:inverse
# \# 8. Don't make anything reproducible

---
class:inverse
# \# 9. Make everything reproducible, and then overwrite your data

---
class:inverse
# \# 10. No one is that interested in your data, so why share it?
